title: Do not hardcode secrets or long-lived credentials
text: Hardcoded tokens, passwords, API keys, and similar credentials create rotation, exposure, and audit problems. Prefer runtime-provided secrets with narrowly scoped access.
summary: Secret-like literals should live in secret management or deployment configuration, not in source.
embedding_text:
rule_no: SEC.1
pack_id: shared_security
language_id: shared
context_id: 
dialect_id: 
section: SEC
title: Do not hardcode secrets or long-lived credentials
summary: Secret-like literals should live in secret management or deployment configuration, not in source.
keywords: not, secrets, credentials, sec.1, hardcode, long-lived, secret-like, literals, should, live, secret, management
category: security
reviewability: auto_review
trigger_patterns: hardcoded_secret
fix_guidance: Move the secret into environment-backed configuration or a secret manager and inject it at runtime.
text: Hardcoded tokens, passwords, API keys, and similar credentials create rotation, exposure, and audit problems. Prefer runtime-provided secrets with narrowly scoped access.
fix_guidance: Move the secret into environment-backed configuration or a secret manager and inject it at runtime.

title: Build shell commands from trusted primitives, not concatenated strings
text: Command construction through string concatenation is fragile and exposes injection risk. Prefer argument vectors, explicit allow-lists, or dedicated APIs.
summary: Shell execution paths should not interpolate untrusted fragments into a command string.
embedding_text:
rule_no: SEC.2
pack_id: shared_security
language_id: shared
context_id: 
dialect_id:

title: Build shell commands from trusted primitives, not concatenated strings
summary: Shell execution paths should not interpolate untrusted fragments into a command string.
keywords: shell, not, command, sec.2, build, commands, trusted, primitives, concatenated, strings, execution, paths
category: security
reviewability: auto_review
trigger_patterns: shell_injection
fix_guidance: Use structured subprocess or exec APIs that separate the executable from validated arguments.
text: Command construction through string concatenation is fragile and exposes injection risk. Prefer argument vectors, explicit allow-lists, or dedicated APIs.
fix_guidance: Use structured subprocess or exec APIs that separate the executable from validated arguments.

title: Parameterize SQL instead of composing query text dynamically
text: SQL should be parameterized through the database driver's bind facilities or a higher-level abstraction. Dynamic text assembly should be reserved for carefully controlled structural clauses.
summary: Query text built from variables is difficult to reason about and easy to misuse.
embedding_text:
rule_no: SEC.3

context_id: 
dialect_id: 
section: SEC
title: Parameterize SQL instead of composing query text dynamically
summary: Query text built from variables is difficult to reason about and easy to misuse.
keywords: text, sql, query, should, sec.3, parameterize, instead, composing, dynamically, built, variables, difficult
category: security
reviewability: auto_review
trigger_patterns: sql_injection, dynamic_sql
fix_guidance: Bind values separately from the SQL string and constrain structural query changes through allow-listed fragments.
text: SQL should be parameterized through the database driver's bind facilities or a higher-level abstraction. Dynamic text assembly should be reserved for carefully controlled structural clauses.
fix_guidance: Bind values separately from the SQL string and constrain structural query changes through allow-listed fragments.

title: Do not leave fallback secrets or test credentials in production code paths
text: Even when intended only for local development or bootstrap, embedded fallback secrets create a second and weaker trust path that is easy to forget during incident response or key rotation.
summary: Backup credentials often become the least-audited credential in the system.

category: security
reviewability: auto_review
trigger_patterns: hardcoded_secret
fix_guidance: Remove embedded fallback credentials and require runtime configuration or a dedicated local-only development path instead.
text: Even when intended only for local development or bootstrap, embedded fallback secrets create a second and weaker trust path that is easy to forget during incident response or key rotation.
fix_guidance: Remove embedded fallback credentials and require runtime configuration or a dedicated local-only development path instead.

title: Keep command execution APIs structured so the executable and arguments remain distinct
text: Separating the executable from validated arguments narrows the injection surface and makes the trust boundary visible in code review across languages and runtimes.
summary: Security review is much stronger when command construction never depends on ad hoc shell parsing.

category: security
reviewability: auto_review
trigger_patterns: shell_injection
fix_guidance: Use structured process APIs, validate each argument according to its contract, and avoid passing shell fragments through string concatenation.
text: Separating the executable from validated arguments narrows the injection surface and makes the trust boundary visible in code review across languages and runtimes.
fix_guidance: Use structured process APIs, validate each argument according to its contract, and avoid passing shell fragments through string concatenation.

title: Dynamic query structure should stay allow-listed even when values are parameterized
text: ORDER BY fragments, table identifiers, and optional clauses can still create injection or correctness risk when the allowed structures are not tightly bounded.
summary: Parameter binding protects values, not uncontrolled structural SQL changes.

reviewability: auto_review

fix_guidance: Keep structural variation behind a small allow-list and continue binding values separately from the SQL text.
text: ORDER BY fragments, table identifiers, and optional clauses can still create injection or correctness risk when the allowed structures are not tightly bounded.

title: Example, bootstrap, and fallback credentials in committed code should be treated as real secrets unless clearly impossible to activate
text: Backup and bootstrap secrets often become the least-audited authentication path in a system because everyone assumes they are temporary or unreachable.
summary: "Test" or "dev" labels do not remove rotation, misuse, or confusion risk once credentials are committed.

summary: "Test" or "dev" labels do not remove rotation, misuse, or confusion risk once credentials are committed.
keywords: bootstrap, credentials, secrets, sec.7, example, fallback, committed, code, should, treated, real, unless
category: security
reviewability: auto_review
trigger_patterns: hardcoded_secret
fix_guidance: Remove committed fallback credentials or move them into an isolated local-only development path that cannot ship as a production default.
text: Backup and bootstrap secrets often become the least-audited authentication path in a system because everyone assumes they are temporary or unreachable.

title: Do not use mutable objects as default argument values
text: A mutable default argument is allocated once at function definition time, so later calls share the same object even when the caller expects a fresh value.
summary: Mutable defaults leak state across calls and create action at a distance.
embedding_text:

pack_id: pep8_python
language_id: python
context_id: 
dialect_id: 
section: PY
title: Do not use mutable objects as default argument values
summary: Mutable defaults leak state across calls and create action at a distance.
keywords: mutable, default, argument, calls, py.1, not, objects, values, defaults, leak, state, across
category: memory
reviewability: auto_review
trigger_patterns: mutable_default
fix_guidance: Use None as the default and allocate the mutable object inside the function body.
text: A mutable default argument is allocated once at function definition time, so later calls share the same object even when the caller expects a fresh value.
fix_guidance: Use None as the default and allocate the mutable object inside the function body.

title: Avoid bare except blocks
text: A bare except makes failure handling too broad for most production code. Prefer catching the narrow exception family the code can recover from.
summary: Catching every exception class hides interruption, cancellation, and programming errors.

category: error_handling
reviewability: auto_review
trigger_patterns: bare_except
fix_guidance: Catch the specific exception type you intend to handle and let unrelated failures surface.
text: A bare except makes failure handling too broad for most production code. Prefer catching the narrow exception family the code can recover from.
fix_guidance: Catch the specific exception type you intend to handle and let unrelated failures surface.

title: Exception handlers should preserve traceback or re-raise intentionally
text: A broad handler can be acceptable at the edge of a subsystem, but the code should still make it obvious which failures are transformed, logged, or re-raised.
summary: Recovery blocks are safer when they do not accidentally swallow cancellation or programmer error context.

category: error_handling
reviewability: auto_review
trigger_patterns: bare_except
fix_guidance: Catch the smallest relevant exception class and re-raise unexpected failures after adding only the context you truly need.
text: A broad handler can be acceptable at the edge of a subsystem, but the code should still make it obvious which failures are transformed, logged, or re-raised.
fix_guidance: Catch the smallest relevant exception class and re-raise unexpected failures after adding only the context you truly need.

title: Mutable defaults should be created per call, not shared across the function lifetime
text: Python evaluates default arguments once. When the default is a list, dict, or set, later callers inherit prior mutations unless the function creates a fresh object inside the body.
summary: Shared default containers create hidden state coupling between otherwise independent calls.
embedding_text:

context_id:

title: Mutable defaults should be created per call, not shared across the function lifetime

keywords: default, shared, function, py.4, mutable, defaults, should, created, per, call, not, across
category: memory
reviewability: auto_review
trigger_patterns: mutable_default
fix_guidance: Use None or another sentinel default and allocate the mutable object inside the function when needed.
text: Python evaluates default arguments once. When the default is a list, dict, or set, later callers inherit prior mutations unless the function creates a fresh object inside the body.
fix_guidance: Use None or another sentinel default and allocate the mutable object inside the function when needed.

title: except Exception should be treated as a broad recovery boundary, not a routine local guard
text: Broad Exception handlers can obscure cancellation, programming errors, and contract drift unless they live at a well-defined translation boundary.
summary: Catching Exception is narrower than bare except but still often too wide for ordinary application logic.

title: Prefer context managers for file and process lifetime
text: Files, sockets, and similar resources are safer when their release is guaranteed by a context manager instead of depending on later cleanup code.
summary: Resources that need closing should usually be bound to lexical scope.

category: resource_management
reviewability: auto_review
trigger_patterns: open_without_context
fix_guidance: Use with-statements or an explicit context manager helper so cleanup is tied to scope exit.
text: Files, sockets, and similar resources are safer when their release is guaranteed by a context manager instead of depending on later cleanup code.
fix_guidance: Use with-statements or an explicit context manager helper so cleanup is tied to scope exit.

title: Treat shell=True and eval/exec as high-trust-only operations
text: Dynamic execution crosses a strong trust boundary. It should not be the default mechanism for normal request handling or data transformation.
summary: Dynamic shell and code execution should be exceptional, tightly bounded, and clearly justified.

category: security
reviewability: auto_review
trigger_patterns: subprocess_shell_true, eval_exec
fix_guidance: Prefer structured subprocess argument lists, parsers, or dedicated dispatch tables over dynamic execution.
text: Dynamic execution crosses a strong trust boundary. It should not be the default mechanism for normal request handling or data transformation.
fix_guidance: Prefer structured subprocess argument lists, parsers, or dedicated dispatch tables over dynamic execution.

title: File and socket lifetime should be bound immediately after acquisition
text: Review Python I O so acquisition, ownership, and cleanup remain in the same small region. That pattern makes it harder for later edits to add a new exit path that leaks the resource.
summary: Resource cleanup becomes fragile when ownership is deferred to later branches in the function.

reviewability: auto_review
trigger_patterns: open_without_context
fix_guidance: Use a context manager or register the cleanup immediately after the successful open when ownership truly must escape.
text: Review Python I O so acquisition, ownership, and cleanup remain in the same small region. That pattern makes it harder for later edits to add a new exit path that leaks the resource.

title: Structured subprocess invocation should separate executable and validated arguments
text: Python subprocess APIs are safest when the executable, arguments, and environment are all explicit values rather than one command string that shell parsing must reinterpret.
summary: Shell-style command strings are a poor default boundary for automation code.

category: security
reviewability: auto_review
trigger_patterns: subprocess_shell_true
fix_guidance: Pass an argument list, validate untrusted fragments before inclusion, and reserve shell=True for tightly audited compatibility cases.
text: Python subprocess APIs are safest when the executable, arguments, and environment are all explicit values rather than one command string that shell parsing must reinterpret.
fix_guidance: Pass an argument list, validate untrusted fragments before inclusion, and reserve shell=True for tightly audited compatibility cases.

title: Prefer parsers, schemas, or dispatch tables over eval and exec for normal control flow
text: Most configuration, transformation, and command selection problems have safer representations than evaluating text as code at runtime.
summary: Dynamic code execution removes static review boundaries exactly where user data often enters the system.

category: security
reviewability: auto_review
trigger_patterns: eval_exec
fix_guidance: Replace eval or exec with an explicit parser, a schema-driven conversion step, or a named dispatch map.
text: Most configuration, transformation, and command selection problems have safer representations than evaluating text as code at runtime.
fix_guidance: Replace eval or exec with an explicit parser, a schema-driven conversion step, or a named dispatch map.

title: Runtime validation and user-input checks should not depend on assert statements
text: Assertions can be stripped or reinterpreted by runtime configuration, so application correctness or input validation should not rely on them as the only enforcement boundary.
summary: assert is a debug-time aid, not a durable contract for external input or production invariants.

category: error_handling
reviewability: auto_review

fix_guidance: Replace assert-based validation with explicit guards, raised domain errors, or structured validation helpers for external input and runtime-critical invariants.
text: Assertions can be stripped or reinterpreted by runtime configuration, so application correctness or input validation should not rely on them as the only enforcement boundary.
fix_guidance: Replace assert-based validation with explicit guards, raised domain errors, or structured validation helpers for external input and runtime-critical invariants.

title: yaml.load should be treated as a trusted-input-only boundary
text: Unsafe YAML loading is a parsing trust decision, not just a data-format choice. In most application code, safer loaders should be the default unless the input is tightly controlled.
summary: Generic YAML loading can execute richer object construction semantics than many callers expect.
